templates.title: 30/60/90 Onboarding Plan — New IC
templates.summary: Structured 90-day onboarding plan for a new IC engineer with explicit manager intent, engineer goals, and checkpoints at 30, 60, and 90 days. Each phase shifts from learning to contributing to operating with calibrated independence.
templates.why_it_matters: The first 90 days set the trajectory. New hires who ramp without structure default to the work that's loudest, not the work that compounds. This template forces the manager to pre-stage real work, calibrate on the team's bar, and have honest checkpoints — instead of finding out at month 6 that the new hire isn't operating at level.
when_to_use: Co-authoring with a new IC engineer in their first week
Reviewing weekly during the first 90 days
Anchoring the 30, 60, and 90 day checkpoints
Calibrating early if the engineer is trending off-track at day 60
not_for: New managers (use the manager variant)
Treating the plan as a checklist instead of a relationship
Substituting for real 1:1 conversation with the new hire
source_inspiration: Michael Watkins — The First 90 Days
Lara Hogan — onboarding playbooks
GitLab Handbook — onboarding flows

inputs_required.description: Laptop, accounts, calendar, buddy, first project all pre-staged.

templates.template_body_markdown:
# 30/60/90 Onboarding Plan — New IC

## How to use

This is the structured plan for a new individual contributor's first 90 days. Co-author it with the new hire in their first week, revisit it weekly, and use it as the artifact at the 30/60/90 checkpoints.

The goals shift across the 90 days: at 30 days, the new hire is *learning the system and people*. At 60 days, they're *contributing on real work with support*. At 90 days, they're *operating with calibrated independence*.

This plan is for an experienced IC joining a new team. Adjust expectations earlier for new-grads (longer ramp on technical fluency) and later for senior+ engineers (faster on technical fluency, slower on org/strategic context).

---

## Header

- **Engineer**: 
- **Level**: 
- **Manager**: 
- **Start date**: 
- **30-day check-in date**: 
- **60-day check-in date**: 
- **90-day check-in date**: 
- **Buddy / onboarding partner**: 

---

## Day 1 setup

Manager owns these. Done before the engineer's first day if possible.

- [ ] Laptop, accounts, and access provisioned
- [ ] Calendar invites for week 1 sent (1:1 with manager, intros with team, intros with cross-functional partners)
- [ ] First-week reading list shared (team mission, current OKRs, key docs, recent decisions)
- [ ] Buddy assigned and briefed
- [ ] First small project identified (something shippable in week 2)
- [ ] Codebase access and dev environment instructions
- [ ] Welcome message in team channel ready

---

## First 30 days — Learn the system and the people

### Manager's intent

The new hire should leave day 30 able to:
- Articulate the team's mission and current goals
- Name the key people (team, cross-functional partners, stakeholders)
- Navigate the codebase / system enough to make small changes
- Identify their own first piece of meaningful work

### Goals for the engineer

- **Technical fluency**: clone the repos, run the system locally, ship one small change (typo fix, doc update, or trivial bug fix in week 1; small feature or fix in week 2–4)
- **System understanding**: read the architecture docs, attend system walkthroughs, ask questions
- **People context**: 1:1 with each teammate, intros with cross-functional partners, attend at least one of every recurring meeting
- **Org context**: read the company strategy, current OKRs, recent product launches, and current customer landscape

### Manager actions

- **1:1 cadence**: weekly, 30 minutes, focused on questions and unblocking
- **Week 1 intros**: schedule 30-minute coffee chats with 5–8 key people (immediate team, manager-of-manager, key cross-functional partners)
- **Week 2 first commit**: ensure the small first project is in flight; pair on the PR if needed
- **Week 4 retro**: what's working in onboarding, what's missing, what's confusing

### Day 30 checkpoint — what to discuss

- What's surprised you about the team / company / system?
- What's been hardest to figure out?
- Who do you not yet know that you should?
- What would make the next 30 days more productive?
- Manager: explicit feedback on what you're seeing — strengths, where to focus

---

## Days 30–60 — Contribute on real work with support

### Manager's intent

The new hire should leave day 60 able to:
- Own a meaningful piece of work end-to-end
- Participate effectively in technical discussions and reviews
- Give and receive feedback in the team's normal rhythm
- Identify when they need help and ask for it well

### Goals for the engineer

- **Real ownership**: own a piece of work that ships in this period (a feature, a refactor, a tooling improvement). Not pair-programming on someone else's work.
- **Technical contribution**: write code that gets reviewed and merged. Give code reviews to others.
- **Design participation**: contribute to at least one technical design discussion (write or review an RFC, propose an approach, push back on a proposal)
- **Customer / product context**: understand the customers being served and the product context for the work

### Manager actions

- **1:1 cadence**: weekly, 45 minutes; shifting from "are you blocked" to "how is the work going"
- **Stretch on calibration**: pair on first 1–2 design decisions or PR reviews to calibrate on the team's bar
- **Connect to broader context**: invite to a strategy review, a customer call, a partner meeting
- **Day 45 mini-checkpoint**: informal — is the engineer trending toward day-60 readiness? If not, what's the gap?

### Day 60 checkpoint — what to discuss

- What's the work you're most proud of so far?
- Where are you still feeling stretched?
- Are there places you're holding back that you'd like to push more?
- How is the team feeling to you now?
- Manager: feedback on technical contribution, collaboration, and growth area for next 30 days

---

## Days 60–90 — Operating with calibrated independence

### Manager's intent

The new hire should leave day 90 able to:
- Operate at their level without constant manager check-in
- Have a clear view of what they own going forward
- Have surfaced the work / scope / direction questions they have for the manager
- Be a normal contributing member of the team

### Goals for the engineer

- **Independent ownership**: own and ship work without manager scaffolding
- **Team contribution**: contribute beyond their immediate work — code reviews, design reviews, mentorship of someone newer
- **Strategic engagement**: participate in planning, raise concerns about scope or direction, ask hard questions
- **First feedback cycle**: participate in any review / feedback cycle that occurs

### Manager actions

- **1:1 cadence**: weekly, 30–45 minutes; normal team rhythm
- **Day 90 retrospective**: comprehensive review of the onboarding (see below)
- **Forward planning**: agree on the engineer's scope and growth direction for the next 90 days

### Day 90 retrospective — what to discuss

- Are you operating at the level we hired you for?
- What's the gap between where you are and where you want to be in 6 months?
- What's the gap between where you are and where I want you to be in 6 months?
- How was the onboarding? What worked, what didn't?
- What's your scope and growth focus for days 90–180?
- Manager: comprehensive feedback — strengths confirmed, growth areas, calibration to the level
- Engineer: feedback to manager — what they need from you going forward

---

## Common failure modes

- **No first project ready**: new hires lose week 1 to "what should I work on" if the manager hasn't pre-staged.
- **Manager checks in but doesn't connect**: 1:1s become status updates instead of relationship-building during the most important period.
- **Letting the buddy do the manager's job**: the buddy is for system / team navigation; the manager owns career, expectations, and feedback.
- **Skipping the day-90 retrospective**: turns the 30/60/90 from a structured ramp into theater.
- **Calibrating too late**: if the engineer is off-track at day 60, the day-60 checkpoint is when to address it, not day 90.
- **Manager-of-manager invisibility**: senior+ hires especially benefit from a 30-day skip-level conversation. Schedule it.

---

## Related artifacts

- `onboarding-30-60-90-manager` — the parallel template for new managers
- `1-1-coaching-pack` (prompts) — for the 1:1 conversations across the 90 days
- `performance-review-pack` (prompts) — for the day-90 retrospective if it overlaps with a review cycle
- `interview-loop-design` — to ensure the loop set realistic expectations the new hire is being onboarded against

templates.title: 30/60/90 Onboarding Plan — New Manager
templates.summary: Structured 90-day onboarding plan for a new manager (external hire or internal promotion) with explicit phases: listen (0–30), hypothesize and adjust (30–60), make the bigger calls (60–90). Includes the manager-of-the-manager's actions and skip-level rhythm.
templates.why_it_matters: Manager onboarding fails differently from IC onboarding. New managers either over-act in the first 30 days (changing things based on incomplete understanding) or under-act past day 60 (avoiding hard calls). This template structures the rhythm so they do neither — and gives the manager-of-the-manager the cadence to coach effectively.
when_to_use: Co-authoring with a new manager (external hire) in week 1
Onboarding an internal IC just promoted into management
Anchoring the 30, 60, and 90 day checkpoints
Coaching a new manager who is over-acting or under-acting
not_for: New ICs (use the IC variant)
Promotion from manager to director (different transition; needs its own template)
Substituting for the new manager's relationship with their team
source_inspiration: Michael Watkins — The First 90 Days
Camille Fournier — The Manager's Path on transitions
Lara Hogan — manager onboarding playbooks

templates.template_body_markdown:
# 30/60/90 Onboarding Plan — New Manager

## How to use

This is the structured plan for a new manager's first 90 days — whether they're external (joined from another company) or internal (just promoted from IC).

Manager onboarding fails differently from IC onboarding. New managers tend to either over-act (changing things in the first 30 days based on incomplete understanding) or under-act (avoiding hard calls because they don't yet have credibility). This plan structures the rhythm to do neither.

The phases:
- **Days 0–30**: listen, observe, build trust. Don't change anything material.
- **Days 30–60**: form a working hypothesis about the team and propose small adjustments.
- **Days 60–90**: make the bigger calls if needed, with the team's support and your manager's air cover.

---

## Header

- **Manager**: 
- **Level**: 
- **Their manager (you)**: 
- **Team they're inheriting**: 
- **Start date**: 
- **30-day check-in**: 
- **60-day check-in**: 
- **90-day check-in**: 
- **Coach / mentor (separate from their manager)**: 

---

## Pre-day-1 setup (you, their manager)

- [ ] All standard IC onboarding items done (laptop, accounts, etc.)
- [ ] 1:1s pre-scheduled with each direct report (in week 1)
- [ ] 1:1s pre-scheduled with key cross-functional peers
- [ ] Read-ahead pack: team OKRs, recent reviews, recent retros, current incidents, attrition history, comp band info, current performance issues you know about
- [ ] Briefing on each direct report (your candid read on each, including the "if you do nothing else, know this about X")
- [ ] Briefing on the team's recent history (last 1–2 years of context)
- [ ] Framing of YOUR expectations as their manager — what you'll be watching for in the first 90 days
- [ ] Mentor / coach assigned (someone who is NOT you — typically a peer manager or an outside coach)

---

## Days 0–30 — Listen and observe

### Your intent for them at day 30

- They have an honest read on each direct report
- They understand the team's current rhythm and conventions
- They have built initial trust by NOT changing things prematurely
- They have surfaced the questions and observations they want to take into days 30–60

### Their goals

- **1:1s with each report**: deep listening, not transactions. Use questions like:
  - "What was the team like before I joined?"
  - "What do you hope I do?"
  - "What are you afraid I'll do?"
  - "What would you change if you were me?"
- **Cross-functional 1:1s**: meet every key partner (product, design, data, sales, support, security as relevant)
- **Operational immersion**: attend every recurring team meeting; observe rituals, decisions, and pace
- **Code/system fluency**: read the architecture docs, attend a system walkthrough, ramp enough to follow technical discussions
- **Org reading**: company strategy, recent leadership comms, current org dynamics
- **DO NOT**: change processes, restructure meetings, give performance feedback, make commitments to the team about future direction

### Your (manager) actions

- **Weekly 1:1**: focused on what they're learning, not what they're doing
- **Mid-week-2 check**: are they meeting the right people? Any gap in the briefing?
- **Skip-levels with their direct reports** (week 3 or 4): get an early read on the new manager's landing
- **Pre-day-30 sync with HR partner**: anything surfacing from the team about the transition

### Day 30 checkpoint — what to discuss

- What's surprised you about the team?
- What's surprised you about the company?
- Who's doing well that you didn't expect? Who's struggling that you didn't expect?
- What's your initial read on each direct report?
- What's your initial read on team health, capability, and current commitments?
- What questions do you have for me?
- Your (manager) feedback on how the listening period has gone

---

## Days 30–60 — Form hypothesis and propose adjustments

### Your intent for them at day 60

- They have a working hypothesis about the team's strengths, gaps, and direction
- They have proposed and made small adjustments (not large reorgs)
- They are starting to deliver coaching and feedback to their reports
- They have built enough credibility to be heard on the bigger calls coming

### Their goals

- **Working hypothesis document**: write up (for you, not the team) what they see — team capability, commitment realism, manager-quality of their own management, top 3 risks
- **Small adjustments**: cadence tweaks, meeting consolidation, clarifying ownership on a fuzzy area, restating expectations on something that's drifted
- **Coaching starts**: weekly 1:1s shift from listening to coaching; specific feedback delivered on small things first
- **Cross-functional working relationships**: contribute meaningfully to a cross-functional planning conversation
- **Begin to own commitments**: pick up at least one team commitment they're driving

### Your (manager) actions

- **Weekly 1:1**: shift to "what's your read, what are you doing about it"
- **Day-45 deep-dive**: review their working hypothesis document with them; calibrate
- **Skip-levels (round 2)**: how is the team responding to the new manager's small adjustments?
- **Cross-functional check**: how are they showing up to peer leaders?

### Day 60 checkpoint — what to discuss

- What's your hypothesis about the team's biggest risk?
- What's your hypothesis about the team's biggest opportunity?
- Where are you still uncertain?
- What do you want to do in the next 30 days that you haven't yet?
- Where do you need air cover from me?
- Your (manager) feedback on coaching, prioritization, decision-making

---

## Days 60–90 — Make the bigger calls

### Your intent for them at day 90

- They are operating as the team's manager, not as a temporary visitor
- They have addressed the biggest gap they identified in days 30–60
- They have given feedback on direction, scope, and team composition where needed
- They have a clear plan for the next 90 days that you've reviewed

### Their goals

- **Address the biggest gap**: this might be a hard performance conversation, a scope change, a cross-team renegotiation, a hire or backfill kickoff
- **Operating cadence**: any cadence changes are settled; the team knows what to expect
- **Coaching depth**: deeper feedback to reports; willingness to have hard conversations
- **Strategic engagement**: contribute to the next planning cycle as a full owner
- **Plan for days 90–180**: their proposed direction for the team

### Your (manager) actions

- **Weekly 1:1**: normal cadence; coaching as needed for the bigger calls
- **Day-90 retrospective**: comprehensive review of the onboarding and forward direction
- **Skip-levels (round 3)**: triangulate the team's read at the 90-day mark

### Day 90 retrospective — what to discuss

- What worked in your onboarding? What didn't?
- What's the team's state at 90 days vs. where you found it?
- What hard call still ahead are you avoiding?
- What's your plan for days 90–180?
- Your (manager) comprehensive feedback — credibility built, gaps still to close, scope confirmed
- Their feedback to you — what they need from you going forward

---

## Common failure modes

- **Manager changes too much in days 0–30**: erodes trust before any is built. Your job in days 0–30 is to remind them not to.
- **Manager avoids hard calls past day 60**: usually a coaching gap; their manager (you) needs to push.
- **No mentor**: their manager (you) cannot be the only person they confide in. Assign a peer or external coach.
- **No skip-levels**: you don't know how the new manager is landing without direct signal from their reports.
- **Letting them inherit broken commitments**: review what they're inheriting BEFORE day 1 and be willing to renegotiate with stakeholders if needed.
- **Day-90 reviews that are vibes-based**: use the comprehensive feedback structure; don't wing it.

---

## Related artifacts

- `onboarding-30-60-90-ic` — the parallel template for new IC engineers
- `manager-quality-review` — for honest read on the new manager at the 90-day mark
- `1-1-coaching-pack` (prompts) — for your 1:1 with them across the 90 days
- `skip-level-agenda` — for the round-1, round-2, round-3 skip-levels with their team

templates.title: Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) — Framework & Letter
templates.summary: Three-part PIP toolkit: pre-PIP diagnosis to test whether a PIP is the right instrument, the co-signed letter itself, and the manager's weekly operating plan including mid-checkpoint and end-state branches.
templates.why_it_matters: PIPs are the most consequential people-management instrument a manager wields. Most failed PIPs failed at the diagnosis stage, not the execution stage. This template forces the manager to do the diagnostic work before issuing the letter, sets observable success criteria, and prevents the end-of-PIP conversation from being a surprise to anyone.
when_to_use: When informal coaching has been tried, documented, and has not closed the performance gap
When the gap is material to the team's outcomes and persistent over weeks or months
When you can articulate observable, measurable success criteria for what 'good' looks like in the next 30–90 days
When you have alignment from your manager, HR partner, and (where required) legal
not_for: Cases where you have not yet given direct, specific feedback on the gap
Cases where the underlying issue is role fit, manager fit, or team dysfunction — fix those first, do not paper over them with a PIP
Cases where you have already mentally written off the employee — that is a managed exit, not a PIP, and should follow the separation playbook
Cases where you cannot write down what 'success' looks like in observable terms
source_inspiration: Camille Fournier — The Manager's Path, ch. 5 on managing through difficulty.
Patty McCord — Powerful, on radical clarity in expectations.
Lara Hogan — Resilient Management, on documenting the support side of difficult conversations.
Standard HR practice from US/UK enterprise tech (review by company HR/legal partner is required before issuing).

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) — Framework & Letter

## How to use

This template is for the manager who has decided that informal coaching has run its course and a structured, documented improvement plan is the right next step.

A PIP is a serious instrument. Use it when (a) the gap between current and expected performance is material and persistent, (b) you've already given specific feedback that was understood and not acted on, and (c) you can write down clear, observable success criteria. If any of those three is missing, work on those first — don't paper over them with a PIP.

This template has three parts:
- **Pre-PIP diagnosis** — for you, the manager, before involving the report
- **PIP letter** — the document the report receives, co-signed
- **Operating plan** — your weekly cadence during the PIP period

Run the diagnosis with your manager and HR partner before you draft the letter. Most PIPs that fail in court or in retrospect failed at the diagnosis stage.

---

## Part 1: Pre-PIP diagnosis

### 1.1 Why now, why this person

- **The performance gap, in observable terms**: 
- **When the gap first became material**: 
- **What you have already done about it** (1:1 feedback dates, written notes, training, scope changes): 
- **The report's stated understanding of the gap** (their words, from your most recent feedback conversation): 

### 1.2 Have you ruled out alternative explanations

- **Role fit**: is the work the right work for this person's strengths? 
- **Manager fit**: have they had clear expectations, regular feedback, and unblocked support from you? 
- **Team conditions**: is the team functional — psychologically safe, properly staffed, with reasonable scope? 
- **Personal circumstances** (within what you can responsibly know): is something material happening in their life that explains the dip? 
- **Compensation / level mismatch**: are they compensated and leveled correctly for the scope? 

If the answer to any of these is no or unknown, address it before issuing the PIP.

### 1.3 Outcome you actually want

- **Best case**: 
- **Realistic case**: 
- **Worst case (and what you'll do)**: 

A PIP that you have already mentally written off is not a PIP — it's a managed exit. Be honest with yourself about which one this is, because the structure of the document differs.

### 1.4 Stakeholder alignment

- **HR business partner reviewed**: ☐ Date: 
- **Skip-level / your manager aware**: ☐ Date: 
- **Legal review (if jurisdiction or risk requires)**: ☐ Date: 
- **Comp/equity implications considered**: ☐ 

---

## Part 2: PIP letter (co-signed)

> Hand this to the report in person, in a private setting, after you have read it through together. Do not email it as a first delivery.

### 2.1 Header

- **Employee**: 
- **Manager**: 
- **Period**: [start date] through [end date — typically 30, 60, or 90 days]
- **Date issued**: 
- **HR partner**: 

### 2.2 Statement of purpose

This Performance Improvement Plan is a structured opportunity to address specific performance concerns. Successful completion of this plan returns the employee to good standing. Failure to meet the criteria below may result in further action up to and including termination of employment.

### 2.3 Performance gap

The following gap exists between expected performance for the role and current performance:

| Area | Expected at level | Observed in the last [N] months |
|------|--------------------|---------------------------------|
|      |                    |                                 |
|      |                    |                                 |
|      |                    |                                 |

Specific examples (events, projects, dates):
- 
- 
- 

### 2.4 Success criteria

Success at the end of the PIP period means meeting all of the following criteria. Each criterion is observable and measurable. Anything not on this list is not part of the PIP.

| Criterion | How it will be measured | Cadence of review |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
|           |                          |                   |
|           |                          |                   |
|           |                          |                   |

### 2.5 Manager support

The manager commits to providing the following during the PIP period:

- Weekly 1:1 dedicated to PIP progress, in addition to regular 1:1
- Written feedback after each weekly review
- Removal of [specific blockers, if any]
- Access to [training, mentorship, tooling] as needed

### 2.6 What is not changing during the PIP

- Compensation and benefits
- Reporting line
- Scope (unless explicitly modified above)
- Confidentiality of the PIP itself — do not discuss with peers; we will not discuss with peers

### 2.7 Resources

- HR partner contact: 
- Employee Assistance Program (if applicable): 
- Right to representation per company policy: 

### 2.8 Acknowledgment

Signing acknowledges receipt and understanding of this plan. It does not constitute agreement with the assessment or admission of underperformance.

- Employee signature / date: 
- Manager signature / date: 

---

## Part 3: Operating plan (manager-side)

### 3.1 Weekly review structure (45–60 min)

- Review the success criteria from the letter, one by one
- For each: evidence collected this week, status (on track / off track / unclear), next-week commitment
- Capture the review in writing within 24 hours; share with the report and HR partner
- End by asking: "Is there anything blocking your ability to meet these criteria that we haven't named?"

### 3.2 Weekly written summary (manager → report, copy HR)

```
PIP weekly review — [employee name]
Week [N] of [total]
Date: 

Criteria status this week:
- [Criterion 1]: [on track / off track / unclear] — [evidence]
- [Criterion 2]: ...
- [Criterion 3]: ...

Manager observations: 

Commitments for next week:
- Employee: 
- Manager: 

Next review: [date]
```

### 3.3 Mid-PIP checkpoint (typically week 4 of an 8-week PIP)

Hold an explicit checkpoint with the report and HR partner. Possible outcomes:
- On track to succeed → continue
- Off track but recoverable → reset commitments, document
- Off track and not recoverable → begin transition planning

The mid-checkpoint exists so that the end of the PIP is not a surprise to anyone.

### 3.4 End of PIP — three possible outcomes

**Successful completion**
- Written confirmation that performance now meets expectations
- Return to standard 1:1 cadence
- Plan for sustained performance review at +60 / +90 days

**Extended PIP** (rare; HR-approved only)
- Specific gaps that remain
- New end date and revised criteria
- Documented justification

**Separation**
- Coordinated with HR on timing, comms, and severance
- Day-of plan: who delivers the news, where, when, what the report leaves with
- Internal comms plan for the team — see the separation comms template

---

## Common failure modes (read before drafting)

- **Vague criteria.** "Improve communication" is not a criterion. "Send a written weekly status update by Friday EOD covering [specific elements]" is.
- **Surprise PIP.** If this is the first the report has heard about the concerns, you have a manager-feedback problem, not a performance problem. Pause and address that first.
- **Stacking criteria the role does not require.** A PIP must reflect the actual job. Adding stretch goals as required criteria is a tell that the manager has already decided.
- **No genuine support offered.** A PIP without manager-side commitments reads as a paper trail, and increasingly will be treated as such by HR and legal.
- **Privately discussing the PIP with peers.** Both the manager and the report should treat the PIP as confidential. Talking about it with the team poisons the team and the report.
- **Skipping the mid-checkpoint.** The end-of-PIP conversation should never be a surprise. The mid-checkpoint is where surprises are eliminated.

---

## Related artifacts

- `underperformance-diagnosis-worksheet` — use BEFORE this template to test whether a PIP is the right instrument
- `pip-conversation-pack` (prompt pack) — language for the PIP delivery conversation and weekly reviews
- `separation-comms-template` — for the team comms if the PIP ends in separation
- `performance-review-packet` — for documenting the gap if the PIP follows a recent review

templates.title: Underperformance Diagnosis Worksheet
templates.summary: A 60–90 minute manager-only diagnostic worksheet to determine whether observed underperformance warrants a PIP, a role change, a manager intervention, a structural fix, or simply more evidence-gathering.
templates.why_it_matters: Most failed performance interventions failed at the diagnosis stage. The manager treated a clarity problem as a performance problem, treated a manager-fit issue as a capability issue, or escalated to a PIP without ruling out alternatives. This worksheet forces the diagnostic discipline that prevents wrong-intervention damage.
when_to_use: Before considering a PIP for an underperforming engineer
When you've been carrying a vague concern about a report's performance for 6+ weeks
When peers or stakeholders are flagging concerns and you want to test whether they reflect a real pattern
Before any conversation with HR about formal action
not_for: Sharing with the report being diagnosed — this is the manager's thinking document
Cases where you have fewer than 3 specific observed events in the last 8–12 weeks (gather evidence first)
Replacing HR consultation — this is input to that conversation, not a substitute
source_inspiration: Camille Fournier — The Manager's Path on diagnosing performance issues
Lara Hogan — Resilient Management on alternative-explanation discipline
Standard organizational psychology on attribution error in performance assessment

inputs_required.name: Observed events
inputs_required.description: 3+ specific, dated events from the last 8–12 weeks.

inputs_required.name: Honest self-reflection
inputs_required.description: Willingness to consider that the issue may be clarity, manager fit, or team conditions — not capability.

sections.name: Observed signal
sections.purpose: Force evidence over impression. If you don't have 3+ specific events, stop.
fields: Event list with dates and observable details

sections.name: Alternative explanations
sections.purpose: Test whether the issue is performance or something else (clarity, role fit, manager fit, team conditions, personal, comp/level, capability).

sections.name: Honest diagnosis
sections.purpose: Most likely explanation with confidence level and what would change your mind.

sections.name: Intervention recommendation
sections.purpose: One of seven discrete interventions; don't stack them.
fields: Option A: Coaching and clarity (clarity issue)
Option E: PIP (capability + sustained pattern + ruled out A–D)
Option F: Managed exit (be honest with yourself)

sections.name: Action this week
sections.purpose: Specific next step.
fields: Re-evaluation date if no decision yet

templates.estimated_time: 60–90 minutes for the worksheet; 2–6 weeks for the resulting intervention
templates.template_body_markdown:
# Underperformance Diagnosis Worksheet

## How to use

This is the worksheet you fill out BEFORE deciding whether to issue a PIP, restructure the role, change the manager, or have a frank "is this still the right job for you" conversation.

Most failed performance interventions failed at the diagnosis stage: the manager treated a clarity problem as a performance problem, treated a manager-fit problem as a capability problem, treated a personal-circumstance moment as a sustained pattern. This worksheet forces the diagnosis before the intervention.

Fill it out alone, in writing, before any conversation with HR or your manager. It typically takes 60–90 minutes done well.

---

## Part 1: The observed signal

### 1.1 What you have observed (specific, dated, observable)

- Event 1 (date, what happened, who was affected): 
- Event 2: 
- Event 3: 
- Event 4: 
- Event 5: 

> If you have fewer than 3 specific events from the last 8–12 weeks, **stop**. You don't have a performance pattern; you have an impression. Either gather evidence over the next 4–6 weeks or address whatever is driving the impression directly.

### 1.2 Pattern across events

- Are these events isolated or part of a pattern? 
- Is the pattern getting worse, staying the same, or getting better? 
- Does the pattern affect a specific kind of work, or all of their work? 
- Does the pattern affect specific relationships (a peer, a partner team)? 

### 1.3 What others are observing

- What are peers saying (in retros, 1:1s, peer feedback)? 
- What is the cross-functional partner saying? 
- What is the report saying about themselves? 

---

## Part 2: Alternative explanations

For each of the following, write the honest answer. If any answer is "I don't know," that's important — investigate it before proceeding.

### 2.1 Clarity

- Have I given them clear expectations for this work, in writing? 
- Have I given them feedback when expectations weren't met, specifically? 
- Could a peer at their level read what I've told them and know what "good" looks like? 

> If clarity is the issue, this is a manager problem, not a performance problem. The intervention is restating expectations and feedback, not a PIP.

### 2.2 Role fit

- Is the work actually a fit for their strengths, interests, and trajectory? 
- Has the role changed since they were hired/promoted into it (e.g., scope grew, team shifted)? 
- Would they be a strong contributor in a different role on the team? 

> If role fit is the issue, the intervention is a role change conversation, not a PIP.

### 2.3 Manager fit

- Have I been an effective manager for them specifically (not generally)? 
- Have we had a coaching relationship, or a checking-in relationship? 
- Has anything in my own behavior contributed to the pattern (lack of feedback, inconsistent direction, withdrawal)? 

> If manager fit is the issue, the honest intervention is a manager change or a serious self-correction, not a PIP from the same manager.

### 2.4 Team conditions

- Is the team functional? Psychologically safe? Properly staffed? 
- Has the report been carrying disproportionate load (on-call, mentoring, ambiguous scope)? 
- Are there team dynamics affecting their ability to do their best work (a difficult peer, an unclear collaboration model)? 

> If team conditions are the issue, the intervention is structural, not individual.

### 2.5 Personal circumstance

- Is there a personal situation (within what you can responsibly know) that explains the pattern? 
- Is the duration of the pattern consistent with a temporary disruption vs. sustained issue? 

> Personal circumstances warrant accommodation, not a PIP. Talk to your HR partner.

### 2.6 Compensation / leveling mismatch

- Are they appropriately compensated and leveled for the scope they're being asked to perform? 
- Is the work at their level, above, or below? 

> If they're operating below their level on the work, the issue may be motivation. If above, the issue may be unrealistic expectations.

### 2.7 Capability

- Do they have the skills, tools, and access to do the work? 
- Has the gap been there from the start, or has the bar moved? 
- Is the gap closeable with training, sponsorship, or pairing? 

> If capability is the issue and closeable, the intervention is investment, not a PIP. If not closeable, see role fit.

---

## Part 3: Honest diagnosis

### 3.1 What is the most likely explanation?

After working through Part 2:

- Most likely: 
- Second most likely: 
- What evidence would confirm the most likely?: 
- What evidence would change my mind?: 

### 3.2 Confidence level

- High / Medium / Low confidence in the diagnosis: 
- If Low: what's the next investigation step before any intervention?: 

---

## Part 4: Intervention recommendation

Based on the diagnosis, the intervention should be ONE of the following. Don't stack them.

### Option A: Coaching and clarity (clarity issue)

- Restate expectations in writing
- Schedule weekly written feedback for 4–6 weeks
- Re-evaluate at the end

### Option B: Role change conversation (role fit issue)

- Frame: "I want to talk about whether this role is the right fit for what you do well"
- Possible outcomes: re-scope current role, move to a different team, leave with dignity
- Not a PIP

### Option C: Manager intervention (manager fit issue)

- This may mean a different manager, or it may mean significant self-correction by you
- Talk to your manager and HR partner; this is hard to address from inside the relationship

### Option D: Structural intervention (team conditions issue)

- Address the team-level issue
- Re-evaluate individual performance once the structural issue is addressed

### Option E: Performance Improvement Plan (capability + sustained pattern)

- See `pip-framework-and-letter` template
- Only if you have ruled out A–D and have specific, observable success criteria

### Option F: Managed exit (decision is already made)

- Be honest with yourself — if this is what's happening, don't disguise it as a PIP
- See your HR partner for the separation playbook

### Option G: Continue monitoring (insufficient evidence)

- Set a specific re-evaluation date (4–8 weeks)
- Define what you're watching for
- Don't drift; come back

---

## Part 5: What you'll do this week

- Decision: 
- Action this week: 
- Who I need to talk to (HR, my manager, the report): 
- Re-evaluation date if no decision yet: 

---

## Common failure modes

- **Stacking interventions.** A PIP after a year of unclear feedback isn't a PIP; it's the manager's failure delivered as the report's problem.
- **Skipping the alternative-explanations work.** The fastest path to a wrong intervention is to skip Part 2.
- **Confidence theater.** Marking "high confidence" because the answer is uncomfortable to hold lightly.
- **Bypassing HR.** Diagnosis is yours; intervention design needs HR.
- **Sharing the worksheet with the report.** This is YOUR thinking document, not theirs.

---

## Related artifacts

- `pip-framework-and-letter` — for Option E
- `1-1-coaching-pack` (prompts) — for Option A
- `performance-review-pack` (prompts) — for documenting the gap if it follows a recent review
- `manager-quality-review` — for honest self-assessment if Option C is on the table

templates.title: Build vs. Buy Decision Memo
templates.summary: Structured memo for build-vs-buy-vs-status-quo decisions on capabilities requiring multi-engineer-quarter investment. Forces comparable analysis: full TCO, time-to-value, strategic ownership, reversibility, and the often-skipped status quo option.
templates.why_it_matters: Most build-vs-buy decisions go wrong because build cost is under-estimated (initial dev is a fraction of TCO), buy cost is over-estimated (sticker price isn't TCO), 'strategic' justification papers over weak quantitative analysis, and the option to stay status quo is never seriously evaluated. This memo forces honest comparable analysis across all three.
when_to_use: Decision about a capability requiring 6+ months of engineering effort if built
Vendor decision that would commit 3+ years of operating cost
Platform vs. point solution evaluations
Strategic capability where 'we should just build it' instinct is loud
not_for: Trivial decisions (small features, minor tooling)
Cases where status quo is genuinely impossible (must do something now)
Bypassing security/legal review of vendor decisions
source_inspiration: Standard enterprise architecture build-vs-buy frameworks
Will Larson — Staff Engineer on platform investment decisions
FinOps Foundation — TCO patterns
Marc Andreessen — software is eating the world (and most non-core software is buy)

inputs_required.name: Capability description
inputs_required.description: What we're deciding about, who consumes it, strategic relevance, maturity of need.

inputs_required.name: Honest TCO data
inputs_required.description: Build cost includes 3-year operating; buy cost includes implementation + ongoing + exit.

inputs_required.name: Vendor finalists (if buy is on table)
Status quo cost
inputs_required.description: Cost of doing without, including the compounding workaround tax.

sections.name: The capability
sections.purpose: Define what we're deciding about and why.

sections.name: The three options
sections.purpose: Build, buy, and status quo — each evaluated comparably.
fields: Option A — Build (scope, initial cost, ongoing cost, TCO, time-to-value, capabilities owned/lacked, risks)
Option B — Buy (vendor candidates, TCO, time-to-value, capabilities gained/lacked, risks)
Option C — Status quo (what it means, current cost, 3-year projection, when it breaks, risks)

sections.name: Comparison and recommendation
sections.purpose: Side-by-side; reversibility; strategic vs. commodity check; recommendation.
fields: Recommendation with rationale tied to the analysis

sections.name: Implementation logistics
sections.purpose: If build or buy, the next-step logistics.
fields: If building: lead, team, phasing, success criteria, sunset plan
If buying: vendor selection, implementation owner, integration scope, success criteria, exit plan

sections.name: Common failure modes
sections.purpose: Pre-empt the ways these decisions go wrong.
fields: Build cost under-estimated (operating is the dominant cost)
'Strategic' used to justify weak quantitative case
Vendor TCO doesn't include exit cost
Builder bias when analyst is also the builder

templates.estimated_time: 8–16 hours over 1–2 weeks to draft properly; review by independent reader before approval
templates.template_body_markdown:
# Build vs. Buy Decision Memo

## How to use

This memo structures the decision to build a capability internally vs. buy it from a vendor (or stay status quo). It's intended for capabilities that are non-trivial — typically multi-engineer-quarter to deliver — where the decision is committing 6+ months of engineering effort and 3+ years of operating cost.

The memo's job is not to advocate; it's to force comparable analysis across options so the decision can be made on the merits. Most build-vs-buy decisions go wrong because:

- Build cost is under-estimated (initial dev is a fraction of TCO)
- Buy cost is over-estimated (sticker price isn't TCO)
- "Strategic" justification papers over weak quantitative analysis
- Optionality cost (what if we change our minds in 2 years?) is ignored

Fill out all sections honestly. Include "stay status quo" as a real option, not a strawman.

---

## Header

- **Decision**: build / buy / stay status quo for [capability]
- **Owner**: 
- **Decision deadline**: 
- **Decision-maker / approver**: 
- **Estimated investment**: 
- **Time horizon for analysis** (typically 3 years): 

---

## Part 1: The capability

### 1.1 What capability are we deciding about

- **Capability description**: 
- **Users / consumers internally**: 
- **Strategic relevance**: how core is this to our differentiated value? (Core / supporting / commodity)
- **Maturity of need**: how stable is the requirement? (Stable / evolving / experimental)

### 1.2 Why are we deciding now

- **Trigger**: 
- **Cost of NOT deciding**: what continues to break or be missed without this decision

---

## Part 2: The three options

### Option A — Build internally

- **Scope of build**: what we'd actually build (MVP and target state)
- **Initial development cost** (engineer-weeks, lead time, $)
- **Ongoing operating cost** (engineering time per quarter to maintain, evolve, support)
- **3-year TCO**: 
- **Time-to-value**: when we'd have the capability live
- **Capabilities we'd own**: data, customization, IP, evolution control
- **Capabilities we'd lack vs. buy**: maturity gaps relative to a market-leading vendor
- **Risks**: scope creep, key-person dependency, missed market shifts, opportunity cost on team's other work

### Option B — Buy (vendor)

- **Vendor candidates**: 1–3 finalists if known
- **Total Cost of Ownership** (license + implementation + ongoing operations + escape cost)
  - Year 1: 
  - Years 2–3: 
  - 3-year TCO: 
- **Time-to-value**: when we'd have the capability live
- **Capabilities gained immediately**: maturity, ecosystem, ongoing innovation
- **Capabilities we'd lack vs. build**: customization, data ownership, integration depth
- **Risks**: vendor lock-in, vendor financial health, integration complexity, security/compliance

### Option C — Stay status quo

- **What status quo means**: doing without, manual workarounds, partial coverage
- **Cost of status quo**: ongoing cost of the workaround (engineering time, customer impact, operational risk)
- **3-year cost projection** (the workaround compounds; quantify): 
- **When status quo breaks**: at what scale, growth, or strategic shift this stops being viable
- **Risks**: what happens if status quo runs out before we revisit the decision

---

## Part 3: Comparison and recommendation

### 3.1 Side-by-side

| Dimension | Build | Buy | Status quo |
|-----------|-------|-----|------------|
| Year-1 cost |  |  |  |
| 3-year TCO |  |  |  |
| Time-to-value |  |  |  |
| Strategic ownership |  |  |  |
| Optionality (can we reverse in year 2?) |  |  |  |
| Capability ceiling |  |  |  |
| Risk profile |  |  |  |

### 3.2 Reversibility analysis

- **Build → Switch later**: cost and time to switch from a build to a vendor in year 2 (often high)
- **Buy → Switch later**: cost and time to switch vendors or migrate to build in year 2 (depends on lock-in)
- **One-way doors vs. two-way doors**: which option locks us in, which preserves optionality

### 3.3 Strategic vs. commodity question

- **Is this our differentiator?**: if the capability IS the differentiator, building usually right; if it's commodity, buying usually right
- **Honest assessment**: most teams overclaim the strategic relevance of work they want to build; check this honestly

### 3.4 Recommendation

- **Recommendation**: build / buy / status quo (with vendor name if buy)
- **Rationale tied to the side-by-side**: not advocacy; show the analysis
- **What we're explicitly accepting**: the trade-offs of the recommended option
- **What would change the recommendation**: conditions under which we'd revisit

---

## Part 4: Implementation logistics (if build or buy)

### If building

- **Lead engineer / architect**: 
- **Team**: 
- **Phasing**: MVP, then evolution
- **Success criteria** (when we'd say the build worked): 
- **Sunset plan if build fails**: when we'd accept it isn't working and pivot to buy

### If buying

- **Vendor selection process**: see `vendor-evaluation-pack`
- **Implementation owner**: 
- **Integration scope**: 
- **Success criteria** (when we'd say the buy worked): 
- **Exit plan if vendor fails**: when we'd switch vendors or rebuild

---

## Part 5: Approval

- **Approver**: 
- **Date approved**: 
- **Approval conditions** (any specific commitments tied to approval): 

---

## Common failure modes

- **Build cost under-estimated**: initial development is a fraction of total cost. Include 3-year operating cost (engineers maintaining, evolving, supporting); typically 30–70% of initial dev cost annually.
- **"Strategic" used to justify weak quantitative case**: if the build is strategic, it should still survive a quantitative comparison. If it doesn't, it's not strategic enough to build.
- **Status quo not seriously evaluated**: most teams skip status quo because it's not exciting; often it's the right answer for 1–2 more years while the org figures out the real strategic shape.
- **Optionality ignored**: locking into a build in year 1 that won't be revisitable in year 3 even if better options emerge.
- **Vendor TCO doesn't include exit cost**: if you commit to a vendor, the cost of leaving is part of the cost.
- **Analyst doing the analysis is also the builder**: builder bias toward building. Get an outside reviewer on the analysis.

---

## Related artifacts

- `vendor-evaluation-pack` (prompts) — for the buy-side vendor selection
- `platform-investment-case` (template) — for the build-side investment case
- `roi-business-case` (template) — for the broader ROI framing
- `tco-narrative` (in `vendor-evaluation-pack`) — for the TCO calculation discipline

templates.title: Pre-Mortem Template
templates.summary: Facilitator's template for running a pre-mortem on a plan, strategy, or initiative — before commitment. Imagining the failed future and working backward to surface predictable failure modes the optimistic planning conversation missed.
templates.why_it_matters: Pre-mortems are the cheapest insurance on a strategic plan. They surface failure modes that the regular planning conversation systematically misses because the room is biased toward optimism, politeness, or sunk-cost momentum. Skipping them means the predictable failures hit and the team is surprised.
when_to_use: Before locking a quarterly plan or strategic commitment
Before launching a major initiative (>$1M cost or 10+ engineer-weeks)
Before committing to a customer-promised roadmap item with high downside
When the team's optimism feels disconnected from the team's track record
not_for: Plans already executing (run a mid-flight check instead)
Trivial decisions (a feature, not a strategy)
Cases where the plan owner is not open to adjusting the plan (pre-mortem becomes performative)
source_inspiration: Gary Klein — original pre-mortem methodology (Harvard Business Review)
Daniel Kahneman — Thinking Fast and Slow on planning fallacy
Annie Duke — Thinking in Bets on probabilistic decision-making

inputs_required.name: Plan to pre-mortem
inputs_required.description: The committed-or-about-to-be-committed plan with success criteria.

inputs_required.name: 4–10 participants
inputs_required.description: Plan owner, direct executors, adjacent skeptics, domain veterans, fresh eyes.

inputs_required.name: Separate facilitator
inputs_required.description: NOT the plan owner — their presence suppresses critique.

inputs_required.name: 90-minute meeting
inputs_required.description: Discrete time block; not folded into another meeting.

outputs: Decisions to make NOW with owners and deadlines

sections.purpose: Set up the conditions for honest critique.

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Pre-Mortem Template

## How to use

A pre-mortem is the inverse of a postmortem. Before committing to a plan, the team imagines a future in which the plan has clearly failed, then works backward to identify what predictably went wrong.

It's the cheapest insurance you can buy on a strategic plan, a quarterly commitment, or a major initiative. It surfaces predictable failure modes that the regular planning conversation misses because the room is biased toward optimism (or politeness, or sunk-cost momentum).

Run a pre-mortem **before** the commitment is locked. Hour-and-a-half meeting, 4–10 people, a facilitator who isn't the plan's owner. The facilitator's job is to keep the room in the failed-future frame and prevent the conversation from drifting back to "but we'll handle that."

This template covers facilitation prep, the meeting itself, and the post-meeting synthesis.

---

## Part 1: Pre-meeting setup (facilitator owns)

### 1.1 Plan to be pre-mortemed

- **Plan / initiative being pre-mortemed**: 
- **Owner**: 
- **Time horizon**: 
- **Stated success criteria** (what the plan claims will be true at the end): 
- **Pre-read sent to participants** (1-page plan summary): 

### 1.2 Participants

Pick 4–10 people across these categories:

- **Plan owner**: yes (will mostly listen, defend less, listen more)
- **Direct executors** (will be doing the work): 2–3
- **Adjacent skeptics** (people who'd be affected but aren't on the team): 1–2
- **Domain veterans** (have seen this kind of plan before): 1–2
- **Outside fresh eyes** (no investment in the plan): 1–2
- **Facilitator**: separate person, NOT the plan owner

### 1.3 Facilitator's prep

- [ ] Read the plan in full
- [ ] Identify 2–3 areas where you suspect the plan glosses (helpful as backup probe questions)
- [ ] Brief the plan owner on their job: listen, take notes, defend nothing in the room
- [ ] Brief participants on the framing: "Imagine it's [end date]. The plan failed clearly and visibly. Why?"
- [ ] Set a no-rebuttal rule for the room

---

## Part 2: The meeting (90 minutes)

### Phase 1 — Frame the failed future (5 min)

The facilitator opens with a specific scenario:

> "It's [end date]. We're doing the postmortem on this plan. It failed — the success criteria were not met, the executive review was bad, and the team is doing damage assessment. For the next 90 minutes, we're working backward from that failure to identify why."

Two ground rules:
1. **No rebuttals.** "But we'll mitigate that" is not allowed in the room. Capture the failure mode; mitigate later.
2. **Specificity.** "People left" is not a failure mode. "Maya left in week 3 and the iOS migration lost its only senior reviewer" is a failure mode.

### Phase 2 — Silent generation (15 min)

Each participant writes failure modes silently. Targets:
- Minimum 5 failure modes per person
- Each is specific (named people, projects, events, dates)
- Each is plausible (not "the office burns down")

The facilitator's role here is to keep the room quiet. Do not begin discussing.

### Phase 3 — Round-robin (30 min)

Each participant reads ONE failure mode at a time, rotating. Facilitator captures verbatim. No discussion until all are surfaced.

This phase usually surfaces 30–60 failure modes. Don't filter; capture all.

### Phase 4 — Cluster and rank (20 min)

Group failure modes into clusters (e.g., "key person leaves," "dependency slips," "scope explodes," "wrong product-market fit"). Then rank clusters by:
- **Likelihood**: how likely in the time horizon (rare / possible / likely / expected)
- **Impact**: if it happens, how bad (minor / material / fatal)

Identify the top 3–5 clusters by likelihood × impact.

### Phase 5 — Hidden dependencies and single points of failure (10 min)

Specific question to surface what the plan rests on:
- "What does this plan secretly depend on that we haven't named?"
- Common: a single person, a partner team's commitment, an external event, an unreviewed decision.

### Phase 6 — Decisions to make NOW (10 min)

For each top cluster, the facilitator asks:
- "What decision could we make in the next week that would meaningfully reduce this risk?"
- Capture the decision, the owner, and the deadline.

The plan owner doesn't have to commit in the room — but every decision should have a named owner and a date for the call.

---

## Part 3: Post-meeting synthesis (24 hours)

The facilitator writes the pre-mortem report:

### 3.1 Top 5 failure clusters (ranked)

For each:
- **Cluster name**: 
- **Specific failure modes** (the verbatim ones from the meeting): 
- **Likelihood × impact rationale**: 
- **Hidden dependency / single point of failure** (if any): 

### 3.2 Decisions to make NOW

| # | Decision | Owner | Deadline | Status |
|---|----------|-------|----------|--------|
| 1 |          |       |          |        |
| 2 |          |       |          |        |

### 3.3 Plan adjustments recommended

- **Cuts** (parts of the plan that should be removed to reduce risk): 
- **Phasing** (work that should happen in a different order): 
- **Mitigations** (specific work added to reduce risk of top clusters): 
- **Acceptance** (risks the team accepts as-is, with explicit acknowledgment): 

### 3.4 Plan changes the owner has agreed to

The owner reviews and either accepts or pushes back, in writing. The pre-mortem only adds value if the plan changes (or the team explicitly accepts the risks).

### 3.5 Scheduled re-check

When (typically a third of the way through the plan) we re-check: are any of the top failure clusters showing early signs?

---

## Common failure modes (in the pre-mortem itself)

- **Plan owner runs the meeting**: their presence subtly suppresses honest critique. Get a separate facilitator.
- **Rebuttals allowed**: "but we'll handle that" rationalizes away the value. The facilitator must enforce no-rebuttal.
- **Generic failure modes**: "communication breaks down," "team is too small." These are useless. Force specificity.
- **No decisions on the way out**: a pre-mortem without decisions is therapy. The Phase 6 step is non-negotiable.
- **No re-check scheduled**: the predicted failure happens in week 7 and no one was watching.
- **Pre-mortem becomes performative**: if the team doesn't believe their input changes the plan, they stop bringing real failure modes. Run pre-mortems only when you genuinely intend to adjust.

---

## When NOT to run a pre-mortem

- The plan is already executing (run a mid-flight check instead)
- The team has already done a thorough risk review (don't duplicate)
- The plan owner is not open to changing the plan (pre-mortem will be performative)
- For trivial decisions (don't pre-mortem a feature; pre-mortem a strategy or a quarter)

---

## Related artifacts

- `pre-mortem-facilitator-pack` (prompts) — for facilitating the meeting and synthesizing the output
- `quarterly-planning-packet` — the plan that often warrants a pre-mortem
- `risk-register` — where the surviving risks land for ongoing tracking
- `dependency-risk-review` — for the hidden-dependency portion at depth

templates.title: Major-Incident Exec Comms Template
templates.summary: Structured exec-facing communication for P0/P1/SEV1 incidents — first notification, periodic updates, resolution comms, post-incident summary, and customer-facing drafts. Calm, specific, honest; commit to next update rather than resolution time.
templates.why_it_matters: During a major incident, the comms are often what determine how the incident is remembered. Slow first notification, speculation as fact, optimistic resolution times, and silent missed updates erode trust permanently. This template gives the manager the structure to communicate with discipline when the room is on fire.
when_to_use: P0/P1/SEV1 incident declared and exec notification needed within 15–30 minutes
During active incident — periodic updates every 30–60 minutes
At resolution — final notification with initial root cause
24 hours later — exec summary for those who need the picture
not_for: Lower-severity incidents (use standard incident comms; this is for major-incident discipline)
Replacing the customer-facing status page workflow
Public PR comms without coordination with PR/Comms team
source_inspiration: Google SRE — incident response and customer communication
PagerDuty Incident Response docs
Atlassian incident management handbook
John Allspaw — Learning from Incidents

sections.name: First exec notification (within 15–30 min)

sections.name: Periodic updates (every 30–60 min)
sections.purpose: Calm, specific, honest. Distinguish know vs. suspect.
fields: Subject line format with update number
Body template (status, scope changes, what's new, what's unknown, mitigation, customer comms status, next update)
Tone notes (no platitudes, no speculation as fact, commit to next update not resolution)

sections.purpose: Final notification when fully resolved (not just mitigated).

Why mitigation took X minutes (if material)

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Major-Incident Exec Comms Template

## How to use

This template is for exec-facing communication during and after a P0/P1/SEV1 incident — when the CEO, CFO, board, or major customers need to know what's happening, what we're doing, and what's true now.

It's NOT the team's incident channel. It's NOT the customer-facing status page (though it informs it). It's the structured comms that go to people who don't have the time or context to read the incident channel.

The principles:
- **Calm, specific, honest.** No platitudes; no panic.
- **Lead with current state.** Not the timeline. Execs need to know what's true RIGHT NOW.
- **What we know vs. what we suspect.** Always distinguish.
- **Commit to the next update time, not the resolution time.** Resolution is uncertain; the next update is a commitment you can hold.

---

## Part 1: First exec notification

> Sent within 15–30 minutes of P0/P1 declaration. The exec hears from you BEFORE they hear from a customer or the press.

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1] [Service / area] — first notice

**Body**:

```
Status: SEV1 declared at [time UTC] — incident in progress.

Current scope:
- Affected: [user count / geography / service]
- Customer-visible impact: [what users experience right now]

What we know:
- [Confirmed fact 1]
- [Confirmed fact 2]

What we don't yet know:
- Root cause is still being determined
- Full scope of impact is being assessed

What we're doing:
- [Active mitigation, in plain language]
- Incident commander: [name]

Next update: [specific time, typically 30–60 min later]

[Your name], [Your role]
```

### Distribution

- Direct line manager (typically VP of Eng → CTO → CEO chain)
- Customer-facing leadership (Sales VP, CS VP, Support lead)
- Comms / PR if customer-visible
- Legal if data is involved

---

## Part 2: Periodic updates (every 30–60 minutes during active incident)

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1] [Service / area] — Update [N] at [time]

**Body**:

```
Status: SEV1 — [active / mitigating / monitoring]
Time since start: [duration]

Current scope:
- Affected: [user count / geography / service — has this changed?]
- Customer-visible impact: [current state]

What we know now (changes from last update):
- [New confirmation 1]
- [Changed assessment]

What's still unknown:
- [Remaining unknowns]

Mitigation in progress:
- [Specific actions, who's doing what]
- [Expected timing for any in-flight action]

Customer comms status:
- Status page: [current message + last updated]
- Direct customer outreach: [tier-1 customers contacted: yes/no]
- Public comms (PR/social): [needed: yes/no/escalating]

Next update: [specific time]

[Your name]
```

### Tone notes

- If the situation is bad, say so. Not "experiencing some issues" when checkout is down for 60% of users.
- If you don't know the cause yet, say so explicitly. Don't speculate as fact.
- Avoid optimistic resolution timing. Commit to the next UPDATE, not the resolution.

---

## Part 3: Resolution comms

> Sent when the incident is fully resolved (not just mitigated).

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1 RESOLVED] [Service / area]

**Body**:

```
Status: RESOLVED at [time UTC]
Total duration: [start to fully resolved]

What happened:
[2–3 sentences in plain language]

Customer impact:
- [Specific scope: # users, regions, services]
- [Revenue / SLA implications if applicable]
- [Customer comms sent: status page, direct outreach, public]

Initial root cause (subject to postmortem):
[What we know now; mark as initial]

What's still open:
- Postmortem due [date]
- Action items (initial list): [3–5 highest-priority]
- Customer follow-up: [what we owe customers]

[Your name]
```

---

## Part 4: Post-incident exec summary (24 hours after resolution)

> A more detailed summary for execs who need the picture without reading the postmortem.

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1 SUMMARY] [Service / area] — [date]

**Headline** (2 sentences): What happened, current status, customer impact.

**Timeline** (compressed, key events with timestamps):
- [Detection]
- [Escalation]
- [Mitigation start]
- [Mitigation effective]
- [Full resolution]

**Customer impact** (specific):
- Users affected: 
- Geography: 
- Services: 
- SLA impact: 
- Revenue impact (if quantifiable): 
- Top-tier customers contacted: 

**Initial root cause**:
[What we know now; postmortem will sharpen]

**Why detection took [X] minutes**:
[Honest about detection lag if material]

**Why mitigation took [X] minutes**:
[Honest about mitigation lag if material]

**Immediate corrective actions taken**:
1. 
2. 
3. 

**Postmortem**:
- Scheduled: [date]
- Lead: [name]
- Will cover: full timeline, root cause, action items, patterns vs. recent incidents
- Will be shared with: [audience]

**What we're committing to**:
- [Specific commitments to leadership / customers / board]

**[Your name]**

---

## Part 5: Customer-facing comms (drafts for each channel)

### Status page

**During**:
> [Service] — investigating elevated [error rate / latency] affecting [scope]. Updates every [N] minutes.

**Resolved**:
> [Service] — issue resolved at [time]. [One-sentence on cause]. Postmortem to follow within 7 days.

### Tier-1 customer email (CSM-driven)

> [Customer name],
> 
> I wanted to reach out personally to let you know about an incident that affected [their use of the service] from [start] to [end]. 
> 
> Specifically: [what they experienced]. 
> 
> The cause was [plain-language summary]. We have [mitigated / fixed] this, and we're conducting a full postmortem this week. I will share that postmortem with you once complete and walk through the prevention work we're committing to.
> 
> If you'd like a call to discuss, please let me know — I'm happy to set up time with [our engineering lead].
> 
> [CSM / executive name]

### Public comms (PR, social — only if customer-visible at scale)

Coordinate with PR/Comms team. Default tone:
- Acknowledge the issue specifically, not vaguely
- Apologize for the impact, not for "any inconvenience"
- State what you're doing to prevent recurrence
- Don't over-promise

---

## Common failure modes

- **Slow first notification**: execs hear about the incident from a customer, the press, or social media before they hear from engineering. Loss of trust is permanent.
- **Speculation as fact**: stating a suspected cause as confirmed; later having to retract. Distinguish "what we know" from "what we suspect" every time.
- **Optimistic resolution time**: "We expect this resolved in 10 minutes" — repeated three times across two hours. Commit to the next update, not the resolution.
- **Update silence**: missing the committed next-update time without notice. If you can't update at the committed time, send a 1-line note that you're delaying the update.
- **Different stories to different audiences**: tier-1 customer hears one thing, the board hears another. Single source of truth.
- **No postmortem follow-through**: promised postmortem in 7 days; delivered in 28. Erodes the credibility of the original comms.

---

## Related artifacts

- `incident-summary-pack` (prompts) — for drafting status updates and post-incident summaries
- `incident-review-packet` — for the full postmortem after resolution
- `escalation-brief` — for non-incident escalations
- `postmortem-action-tracker` — for following through on the action items
